source_article:
# Intent Engineering: The Missing Discipline in AI Agent Development

*I built a tool to orchestrate AI pipelines — content, code, security audits, anything with phases and judgment calls. What I learned along the way explains why Klarna saved $60M and rehired anyway — and why 40% of agentic AI projects are about to be canceled.*

---

I am a day-one ChatGPT user who spent the better part of three years building what a reasonable person might describe as the world's most elaborate LinkedIn post generator.

That's not actually what it became. And the gap between those two descriptions is exactly what this article is about.

Let me back up.

When ChatGPT launched, I was there on day one. Not watching from a distance — using it. In my work, immediately. The discipline everyone was discovering was prompt engineering: if you gave the model the right words, in the right order, with the right structure, you got something genuinely useful back. I got good at it fast. You feel like a genius when a well-crafted prompt returns something that would have taken you two hours to write. The blast radius of a bad prompt is exactly one interaction. Quick feedback. Immediate results. Addictive.

That era was real and valuable. And it turned out to be only the beginning.

---

## Every Tool I Built Showed Me What the Previous Tool Was Missing

The context engineering revelation hit me the way most important insights do: through a specific, undeniable moment of "oh, *that's* what's actually happening here."

The concrete moment that made it undeniable for me: I had a project that required over 100 user stories. The kind of task that would take a full day manually. I used Gemini, uploaded the relevant project context, built the right prompting structure, and even got CLI tools to batch-upload the outputs. Done in an hour. The model wasn't smarter than the alternatives. The architecture was right — the right information, organized correctly, present in the window at the right time. That was the shift.

When the new generation of models arrived with dramatically larger context windows, I understood why at a deeper level.

Andrej Karpathy named this cleanly in June 2025: *"Context engineering is the delicate art and science of filling the context window with just the right information for the next step."* ([Karpathy, X, June 2025](https://x.com/karpathy/status/1937902205765607626)) Karpathy naming a thing makes it real. This had been the right instinct all along — we just finally had the right words for it.

So I went from optimizing prompts to optimizing architectures. And eventually I decided to build something: a YAML-based pipeline system for AI workflows. Think Docker Compose, but for AI pipelines. I call it the orchestration engine. It's been in active development for about three weeks now, since early February. It's not done — probably another week or two, maybe a month at most. But what I've learned building it is what this article is actually about.

The content pipeline is the example I'll use here because it's the most intuitive — but the engine runs coding pipelines (requirements → implementation → code review → fixes → tests), security audits (threat modeling → code analysis → vulnerability assessment), UX audits, research pipelines, and anything else you can decompose into phases with different model tiers and judgment requirements. The architecture is domain-agnostic. The intent problem shows up everywhere.

Here's the thing about building a tool: you learn what the tool can't do. Each new feature didn't solve the problem — it made the *next* problem visible. I added a research agent. Then I needed a writing agent. Then a fact-checking agent. Then a revision agent. Then, after the translation incident, a red team agent.

(The red team agent's entire job is to argue with the other agents. At some point I realized I had not built a content pipeline. I had built a constitutional democracy. It turned out that was the right call.)

The translation incident deserves a sentence or two. I wrote an article about AI and literary translation. It got 52 comments, many of them from professional translators who had opinions. Strong ones. Well-founded ones. The article had gaps I hadn't designed for — shallow source verification, no adversarial review step, confidence where humility was warranted. I wrote "Building With The Bricks They Throw" about what I learned from that backlash. And then I rebuilt the pipeline.

The red team phase exists because of that incident. The rules you add after an incident are the most honest intent specifications you'll ever write. They're not aspirational. They're empirical. (The 24-hour cooldown rule exists for related reasons — call it the general principle of "things I was certain about that turned out to be wrong.")

---

## Klarna Did Everything Right. Then Hired Everyone Back.

The red team incident was a small-scale version of a problem that Klarna ran into at a scale of 853 agents and $60 million. The shape of the failure is identical. Before I name the pattern, let me give you the industrial version.

In January 2024, Klarna deployed an AI customer service agent that was, by any engineering metric, a triumph. In its first month, it handled 2.3 million conversations. Resolution time dropped from 11 minutes to 2 minutes. By Q3 2025, the system had scaled to the equivalent of 853 full-time agents and represented an estimated $60 million in labor cost avoidance compared to hiring at scale. ([Nate Jones, Substack, Feb 24, 2026](https://natesnewsletter.substack.com/p/klarna-saved-60-million-and-broke); [Jason, DEV.to, Feb 25, 2026](https://dev.to/webdevtodayjason/the-ai-race-is-over-a-new-race-has-already-begun-le1))

A note on precision before we go further: that $60M is a counterfactual — what Klarna *would have* spent on human labor, not a reduction on the P&L. The actual customer service cost figures in Klarna's earnings show a more complex picture than the savings headline suggests. The story is still compelling. It's a different story when you hold it precisely.

And then CEO Sebastian Siemiatkowski publicly started rehiring human agents. His own words: *"Really, investing in the quality of human support is the way of the future for us."* ([Entrepreneur, May 2025](https://www.entrepreneur.com/business-news/klarna-ceo-reverses-course-by-hiring-more-humans-not-ai/491396))

The reversal likely reflects multiple things — regulatory pressure, IPO optics, edge case complexity. What we can say is this: the agent's stated goal was "resolve tickets fast." Klarna's actual organizational goal was something closer to "build lasting customer relationships that drive lifetime value." As Jason at DEV.to put it sharply: *"It had a prompt. It had context. It did not have intent."* ([Jason, DEV.to, Feb 2026](https://dev.to/webdevtodayjason/the-ai-race-is-over-a-new-race-has-already-begun-le1))

Human agents absorb intent through years of experience, through stories, unwritten rules, the social consequences of getting it wrong. When you fire 700 of them, that knowledge walks out the door. An AI agent only knows what you've codified. And most organizations have codified far less of their intent than they think.

Nate Jones articulated this in [his YouTube video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QWzLPn164w0) — the video that crystallized what I'd been bumping into for weeks: intent engineering is *"the discipline of making organizational purpose — goals, values, tradeoffs, decision boundaries — machine-readable and machine-actionable so that when you deploy an autonomous system, it optimizes for what your company actually needs, not just what it can measure."*

That's the thing the Klarna agent didn't have. That's the thing most agents don't have.

---

## Three Disciplines, Three Eras — Bear With Me, This Actually Matters

To understand why intent is the missing piece — and why it keeps getting skipped — it helps to see where it sits in the sequence most of us have actually traveled. I'm going to give you a three-era progression here. I know exactly how that sounds. Bear with me — it maps to something real.

**Era 1: Prompt Engineering (2022–2023).** What to *do*. The craft was in the words. One session, one human, immediate feedback. Brilliant for point-in-time tasks. Failed at scale, complexity, and anything requiring sustained coherence across an agent network.

**Era 2: Context Engineering (2024–2025).** What to *know*. Agents, pipelines, RAG stacks, memory systems. LangChain's team defines the four strategies: write (save context outside the window), select (pull relevant context in), compress (retain essential tokens), isolate (split context for parallel tasks). ([LangChain, Oct 2025](https://blog.langchain.com/context-engineering-for-agents/)) Most sophisticated teams are operating here right now. It's genuinely powerful. It still doesn't solve the question Klarna ran into.

**Era 3: Intent Engineering (2026→).** What to *want*.

This isn't a new discipline that makes the previous two obsolete. Intent is already present in context engineering — in system prompts, behavioral guidelines, evaluation criteria. The argument is that it deserves to be *explicit and first-class*, not implicit and accidental.

The sharpest formulation I've found comes from Paweł Huryn at Product Compass: *"Intent is what determines how an agent acts when instructions run out."* ([Product Compass, Jan 2026](https://www.productcompass.pm/p/intent-engineering-framework-for-ai-agents))

Read that again. When the instructions run out. That's the moment everything defaults — to the model's training, to the proxy metric, to the path of least resistance. That's where Klarna's agent optimized ticket velocity instead of customer loyalty.

The numbers make clear this isn't a theoretical concern. MIT found that 95% of enterprise generative AI pilots fail to deliver measurable ROI — not because the AI doesn't work, but because the AI was never aligned to what the business actually needed. ([MIT/Fortune, Aug 2025](https://fortune.com/2025/08/18/mit-report-95-percent-generative-ai-pilots-at-companies-failing-cfo/)) Gartner predicts over 40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by end of 2027, and their primary stated cause is *"unclear business value"* — not technical failure. ([Gartner, June 2025](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2025-06-25-gartner-predicts-over-40-percent-of-agentic-ai-projects-will-be-canceled-by-end-of-2027))

Gartner isn't saying 40% will fail because the AI doesn't work. They're saying 40% will fail because nobody encoded what "working" means for the business. That's the intent gap. With a price tag.

---

## What the 8-Phase Pipeline Is Actually Encoding

Here's where I want to get concrete, because abstract disciplines are easy to nod at and harder to build.

When I describe my content pipeline as having 8 phases, it sounds like workflow automation. It isn't. It's a running implementation of intent engineering. Every phase answers the same question: *When the agent has to choose between two outputs that both satisfy the stated objective — which one do we want? And have we told it?*

"Generate a LinkedIn article" is a prompt. What the pipeline actually encodes is this: *Produce content that builds technical authority, respects the reader's intelligence, survives a domain-expert red team, contains zero fabricated sources, never embarrasses the author, and only publishes when a human who slept on it says yes.* That's intent.

Here's how each phase maps to a specific value decision:

| Phase | What It Does | Intent It Encodes |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **1. Research** | Structured brief, quality-rated sources, primary citations only | Authority over speed. A sourced claim beats a confident one. |
| **2. Writing** | Author voice brief, factual constraints built into the prompt | Brand fidelity is non-negotiable. The AI writes; the voice is mine. |
| **3. Fact-check** | No fabricated quotes or stats; every claim traceable | Trust, once broken with an audience, costs more than any article is worth. |
| **4. Revision — mechanical** | Fast, cheap model handles typos, structure, flow | Know which tasks need which model. Cost and quality aren't always in tension. |
| **5. Revision — editorial** | Expensive model where judgment matters | Not all tokens are equal. Deploy reasoning where reasoning is required. |
| **6. Red team** | Domain expert simulation; condescension flags; "do I know what I don't know?" | Earned after the translation incident. This phase exists because I was wrong. |
| **7. Cooldown** | 24-hour minimum, regardless of confidence level | Quality over urgency. Time changes your mind about things you were sure of. |
| **8. Human review + publish** | I read it before it ships. I decide when done is done. | Human in the loop is a feature, not a fallback. |

The model tier assignments aren't arbitrary, either. The engine uses Claude Haiku 4.5 for mechanical tasks — typos, formatting, structural fixes. Claude Sonnet 4.6 for research and writing, where synthesis and voice matter. Claude Opus 4.6 for the red team and editorial judgment calls, where getting it wrong has actual consequences. Cheap where cheap is right. Expensive where expensive earns its cost.

A few phases deserve more than a table row.

The **red team phase** is the one I had to earn. The question it's designed to answer isn't "is this factually correct?" — the fact-check phase handles that. The question is: *Does this condescend to someone who knows more about this than I do?* That distinction is the heart of what went wrong with the translation article. I wasn't factually wrong about most things. I was wrong about the edge of my own competence. The red team phase forces the question before the audience does.

The **24-hour cooldown** is the simplest phase and probably the one I trust most. The rule is unconditional: regardless of how good it looks, regardless of how sure the agents are, it sits for 24 hours. I've added a lot of phases by reasoning from first principles. This one I added because I noticed that time reliably changes my mind about things I was certain of. That's a different kind of evidence. I trust it accordingly.

The **human review gate** is the most important intent decision in the whole system. The agent does not publish. I do. That distinction is not a limitation of the pipeline. It's the point. Some decisions should stay human — not because AI can't execute them, but because I want to be accountable for what goes out under my name.

The 8-phase pipeline is my answer to that problem. It's imperfect, and it's evolving, and it's mine.

---

## What Intent Engineering Actually Requires

The pipeline is the proof. Here's the theory that explains why it works — and what you'd need to apply it to your own context.

**A value hierarchy.** When agent goals conflict, which wins? Speed vs. thoroughness. Cost vs. quality. Short-term resolution vs. long-term retention. Most teams never write this down. The model resolves conflicts using its defaults, which are optimized for general helpfulness — not your specific organizational values. If you haven't specified which value wins, you've outsourced that decision to a model that doesn't know what you care about.

**A definition of "done" that matches what you actually care about.** A closed ticket is an event. A customer who feels heard is an outcome. A customer who stays loyal is a goal. Most agents are optimized for the event because it's measurable. The goal requires encoding — including what signals the agent uses to evaluate whether it's been achieved.

**Escalation logic treated as values made operational.** Every escalation rule is a statement about what you care about. "Escalate when the customer asks to speak to a human" is one value set. "Escalate when sentiment detects frustration, regardless of explicit request" is another. These aren't neutral technical choices. They're organizational values expressed as code. If you've never asked what your escalation logic is actually saying about what you value — ask that question.

**Explicit error asymmetry.** Not all mistakes cost the same. In my content pipeline, a false positive from the red team — flagging something as problematic when it's fine — costs me a revision cycle. A false negative — missing something that a domain expert would find dismissive — costs me credibility with exactly the audience I'm trying to reach. I've designed the thresholds accordingly. Different organizations, different contexts, different thresholds. But the design should be deliberate, not default.

---

## The Race Has Already Shifted

Deloitte's 2026 technology predictions frame the market moment clearly: the challenge in 2026 isn't whether multi-agent systems work. It's whether they carry coherent intent across agent networks. Their analysis calls for "supervisor agents that interpret requests, route tasks, and manage access" — and notes that these supervisor agents *must carry intent, not just instructions*. ([Deloitte, Nov 2025](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/technology/technology-media-and-telecom-predictions/2026/ai-agent-orchestration.html))

That's precisely what my orchestration engine's pipeline is doing. Not because I anticipated Deloitte's framework — because I kept running into the same wall, kept adding phases that encoded what previous phases couldn't capture, and eventually had to give a name to what I was building.

CIO.com put the ongoing commitment required clearly: *"AI alignment is not a one-time fix, but an ongoing assurance discipline."* ([CIO.com, July 2025](https://www.cio.com/article/4014896/ai-align-thyself.html)) Even a well-designed intent layer drifts. The cooldown and human review gates exist partly for this reason — they're not just quality checks, they're alignment checks.

And Jason at DEV.to put the larger shift in the sharpest terms: *"It is no longer about who has the smartest AI. It is about who has built the organizational infrastructure that lets AI operate with the fullest, most accurate, most strategically correct understanding of what the organization is actually trying to accomplish. This is the intent race."* ([DEV.to, Feb 2026](https://dev.to/webdevtodayjason/the-ai-race-is-over-a-new-race-has-already-begun-le1))

The models are extraordinary — and they're table stakes now. We've been in the context race for a year. Now comes the intent race. And almost nobody is building for it yet.

---

## One More Thing (The Open-Source Part)

I've been building this orchestration engine on a conviction I keep coming back to: every lesson I learn should become a feature. Not just for me. The translation incident became the red team phase. The cooldown rule came from a painful moment. The model tier assignments came from months of watching where cost and quality actually intersect. Those lessons are worth more distributed than hoarded.

So the orchestration engine is going open source. Free, for everyone. It's not done — probably another week or two, maybe a month at most. But the architecture, the content pipeline, the coding pipeline, the security audit templates, the YAML-based multi-model routing, the scenario testing framework — all of it. A Swiss knife for AI pipelines, built from real pain, given away for free.

If that sounds like I'm applying intent engineering to my own publishing pipeline and realizing the intent was always "share what you learn" — yes. That's exactly what happened. The intent specification was always there. I just had to build long enough to make it explicit.

---

## Before Your Next Agent Ships

One question worth forcing into every AI deployment, before it scales:

*If the agent has to choose between two outcomes that both satisfy the stated objective — which one do we want? And have we told it?*

Klarna didn't fail. They built something genuinely impressive and learned something genuinely important at scale. The agent succeeded. It succeeded at the wrong thing. When agents make thousands of consequential decisions per minute, the defaults of the underlying model substitute for organizational intent wherever that intent hasn't been explicitly encoded. Sometimes the defaults are fine. Often they're not. And you usually don't know which until you've already scaled.

The question isn't whether you're building agents. You are. The question is whether you're encoding intent, or hoping context is enough.

What's your intent gap?

---

*Conny Lazo builds AI pipelines and shares what he learns. The orchestration engine mentioned in this article is being open-sourced in the coming weeks. Follow for updates.*

---

**Sources referenced:**
1. Nate Jones, *Intent Engineering* — [YouTube, Feb 24, 2026](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QWzLPn164w0)
2. Jason, *The AI Race Is Over. A New Race Has Already Begun.* — [DEV.to, Feb 25, 2026](https://dev.to/webdevtodayjason/the-ai-race-is-over-a-new-race-has-already-begun-le1)
3. Gartner, *Gartner Predicts Over 40% of Agentic AI Projects Will Be Canceled by End of 2027* — [Gartner, June 25, 2025](https://www.gartner.com/en/newsroom/press-releases/2025-06-25-gartner-predicts-over-40-percent-of-agentic-ai-projects-will-be-canceled-by-end-of-2027)
4. MIT/Fortune, *95% of generative AI pilots at companies are failing* — [Fortune, Aug 2025](https://fortune.com/2025/08/18/mit-report-95-percent-generative-ai-pilots-at-companies-failing-cfo/)
5. Andrej Karpathy, context engineering post — [X, June 25, 2025](https://x.com/karpathy/status/1937902205765607626)
6. LangChain, *Context Engineering for Agents* — [LangChain Blog, Oct 2025](https://blog.langchain.com/context-engineering-for-agents/)
7. Paweł Huryn, *The Intent Engineering Framework for AI Agents* — [Product Compass, Jan 13, 2026](https://www.productcompass.pm/p/intent-engineering-framework-for-ai-agents)
8. CIO.com, *AI, align thyself* — [CIO.com, July 1, 2025](https://www.cio.com/article/4014896/ai-align-thyself.html)
9. Deloitte, *Unlocking exponential value with AI agent orchestration* — [Deloitte 2026 Tech Predictions, Nov 2025](https://www.deloitte.com/us/en/insights/industry/technology/technology-media-and-telecom-predictions/2026/ai-agent-orchestration.html)
10. Entrepreneur, *Klarna Is Hiring Customer Service Agents After AI Couldn't Cut It on Calls* — [Entrepreneur, May 9, 2025](https://www.entrepreneur.com/business-news/klarna-ceo-reverses-course-by-hiring-more-humans-not-ai/491396)
editorial_brief:
EXPAND this article with the following additions. The article is already excellent — evolve it, don't rebuild it.

## 1. Holistic Engine Framing
The orchestration engine is NOT just for content. Expand the scope explicitly: coding pipelines (requirements → implementation → code review → fixes → tests), security audits, UX audits, image generation pipelines, movie direction — anything decomposable into phases with judgment requirements. The architecture is domain-agnostic. Show this with brief examples beyond content.

## 2. Intent as a Framework Feature
Each pipeline step carries the mission, core message, and author voice. Intent isn't just philosophy — it's architecture. The YAML definition IS the intent specification. Make this concrete: show how a phase prompt in YAML encodes intent that would be scattered across Python files in LangGraph or CrewAI.

## 3. Competitive Landscape
Weave in the competitive analysis naturally. Key points:
- Promptflow (closest YAML competitor) was killed by Microsoft Oct 2025. The market space is empty.
- LangGraph/CrewAI are code-first — intent lives in Python, not in readable YAML
- Zero competitors have scenario testing or quality gates as first-class architecture
- Position as 'Docker Compose for AI pipelines'
- Don't trash competitors — acknowledge strengths honestly, claim our genuine differentiation

## 4. AI Bias Argument
When you give a single AI one task to do everything, it creates its own bias. The model reinforces its own patterns. A pipeline with checks and balances — separate research agent, separate fact-checker, separate red team, separate editor — mitigates this structurally. This is like separation of powers in government. Different agents with different roles prevent any single perspective from dominating.

## 5. Humor (Jennifer Aaker framework)
Add levity throughout. The article is very serious. Inspiration: Jennifer Aaker (Stanford GSB) — humor builds trust and perceived competence. Self-deprecating humor outperforms bragging.
Examples of the energy we want:
- '2100 tests. Most of them pass.' (confident self-awareness)
- The 'Rube Goldberg machine' energy already in the article — more of that
- 'Have you tried reading it?' (puncturing complexity with simplicity)
- Brief asides, not forced joke sections

## 6. Dark Factory Connection
Link to the StrongDM Dark Factory methodology and the existing blog article on connylazo.com. The Dark Factory model (humans define intent → agents execute → scenario-based validation) validates our entire architecture thesis. Our engine IS the infrastructure layer for this model.

## 7. Scenario Runners
Expand on the testing framework. 2100+ tests. Scenario-based validation as first-class architecture. This is what makes the engine trustworthy — not just that it runs pipelines, but that it tests them. Connect to StrongDM's methodology.

## TONE RULES
- Builder sharing what he learned — NOT defending, NOT apologizing
- Humor should feel organic, never forced
- Acknowledge competitor strengths honestly
- Keep the first-person voice throughout
- The article should feel like talking to a smart friend who built something interesting
research_data:
# Competitive Analysis: AI Orchestration Engines
**Phase:** competitive_analysis  
**Pipeline:** research-competitive  
**Date:** 2026-02-26  
**Status:** SELF-BOOTSTRAPPED (market_scan.output not injected — see note at bottom)

---

## Executive Summary

The AI orchestration market has fragmented into three camps: (1) **code-first graph engines** (LangGraph, CrewAI) that give full power to Python developers but require significant coding expertise; (2) **visual/low-code platforms** (Dify, Langflow) that lower the barrier but sacrifice composability at scale; and (3) **enterprise consolidations** (Microsoft Agent Framework = AutoGen + Semantic Kernel) tied to vendor ecosystems. **Zero competitors offer YAML-declarative pipeline definition with built-in scenario testing and explicit quality gates.** That gap is wide open and well-aligned with where the market is headed — the StrongDM "Dark Factory" model shows the industry moving toward human-defined-intent + agent-executed + scenario-validated workflows, which is exactly what a YAML-based orchestrator with scenario testing enables.

---

## 1. Feature Comparison Matrix

| Feature | LangGraph | CrewAI | Microsoft Agent Framework (AutoGen) | Dify | Langflow | **Our Product** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline definition** | Python code | Python code | Python code | Visual GUI | Visual GUI | YAML declarative |
| **Intent preservation** | Developer-managed | Developer-managed | Developer-managed | Visual nodes | Visual nodes | Phase-level prompt engineering |
| **Quality gates** | Custom code | Custom code | Middleware hooks | Basic guardrails | None native | Built-in (grader phases) |
| **Scenario testing** | None | None | None | None | None | ✅ Native (2100+ tests) |
| **Multi-model support** | LangChain ecosystem | Any LLM API | Azure-preferred | Many via UI | Many via UI | Anthropic/OpenClaw/Gemini |
| **Bias mitigation** | None native | None native | None native | None native | None native | Red-team/fact-check phases |
| **YAML vs code** | Code only | Code only | Code only | GUI/YAML export | GUI/JSON export | YAML-first |
| **Testing framework** | LangSmith (paid) | None native | None | None | None | pytest-native, 2100+ tests |
| **Learning curve** | High (graph theory) | Medium (roles/tasks) | High (enterprise) | Low (GUI) | Low (GUI) | Medium (YAML) |
| **Documentation quality** | Excellent | Good | Good | Good | Moderate | In progress |
| **Runs on minimal hardware** | No | No | No | No (Docker stack) | No | ✅ Raspberry Pi |
| **Extensibility** | High (Python) | High (Python) | High (Python) | Moderate (plugins) | Moderate | High (YAML + executors) |
| **Pricing/License** | Open source (MIT) + LangSmith SaaS | Open source (MIT) + CrewAI Cloud | Open source | Open source + cloud | Open source | Open source |

---

## 2. Architecture Comparison

### LangGraph (LangChain Inc.)
**Architecture:** Directed acyclic graph (DAG) where nodes = agent actions, edges = control flow decisions. State is explicitly typed and shared across nodes. Supports cycles for loops/retries.  
**Core model:** Python-code-defined state machines with checkpointing. Every "step" is a node; routing logic lives in Python functions.  
**Key insight:** Extremely powerful, but you're essentially writing a state machine from scratch in Python. The mental model is a graph compiler, not a pipeline runner.  
[Source: https://github.com/langchain-ai/langgraph]

### CrewAI
**Architecture:** Role-based — you define "agents" (with persona/role/goal), "tasks" (what to do), and a "crew" (who does what, in what order). Two modes: Crews (autonomous collaboration) vs Flows (explicit step-by-step).  
**Core model:** The abstraction is a team of specialists. Fast execution (claims 5.76x vs LangGraph in some benchmarks). Built entirely from scratch — no LangChain dependency.  
**Key insight:** Best UX for rapid prototyping of multi-agent workflows. But when you need precise control over what happens between steps, Flows are still Python-first.  
[Source: https://github.com/crewAIInc/crewAI]

### Microsoft Agent Framework (AutoGen + Semantic Kernel unified, Oct 2025)
**Architecture:** AutoGen and Semantic Kernel merged into a single framework in public preview Oct 1, 2025. AutoGen and Semantic Kernel are now in maintenance mode (security patches only, no new features). The new framework combines session-based state, middleware, telemetry, type safety, and graph-based workflows.  
**Core model:** Enterprise-grade, Microsoft Azure-native. Best for teams already in the Microsoft ecosystem.  
**Key insight:** The consolidation signals Microsoft's long-term bet, but it also means two popular frameworks are now frozen. Community is migrating.  
[Source: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework/overview/agent-framework-overview, https://cloudsummit.eu/blog/microsoft-agent-framework-production-ready-convergence-autogen-semantic-kernel]

### Dify
**Architecture:** Visual-first no-code/low-code builder. RAG pipeline management, vector database integration, MCP protocol support, workflow nodes. Web-based GUI.  
**Core model:** Build apps via drag-and-drop. Complex agents require code extensions. Strong for teams without coding expertise.  
**Key insight:** Great for launching fast; ceiling hit quickly when you need custom logic or systematic testing. Observability built in but limited.  
[Source: https://dify.ai/, https://sider.ai/blog/ai-tools/dify-review-2025-is-this-open-source-llm-builder-ready-for-production]

### Langflow
**Architecture:** Visual wrapper over LangChain. Flow-based UI for constructing LLM pipelines via drag-and-drop. Exports to Python.  
**Core model:** Similar to Dify but more code-adjacent. Targets developers who want a visual design tool before writing code.  
**Key insight:** Niche — developer-oriented visual design that still requires LangChain knowledge. Less popular than Dify for non-coders, less powerful than LangGraph for coders.

### Promptflow (Microsoft)
**Architecture:** YAML-defined flows for LLM applications, with evaluation built in. **Closest competitor to our YAML approach**, but now in maintenance mode as Microsoft consolidates to Agent Framework.  
**Key insight:** Promptflow proved the market for YAML-defined AI pipelines — and then Microsoft abandoned it. The use case is validated; the incumbent is weakened.  
[Source: https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/blog/educatordeveloperblog/llm-based-development-tools-promptflow-vs-langchain-vs-semantic-kernel/4149252]

### Haystack (deepset)
**Architecture:** Component-based RAG and retrieval framework. Pipelines are Python objects connecting components (readers, retrievers, generators). Python-code-first.  
**Core model:** Best-in-class for RAG and document intelligence. Not a general-purpose agent orchestrator.  
**Key insight:** Narrow focus is its strength and its limit. Not competing directly with us except at the "pipeline" concept level.

---

## 3. Gap Analysis

### What NO competitor does well:

**A. Declarative intent preservation**  
All code-first frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen) make intent implicit — it lives in function names, comments, and developers' heads. When you change a prompt, you touch Python code. When you want to audit what a phase was supposed to do, there's no YAML you can read. Promptflow came close with YAML but is now dead.

**B. Scenario testing as a first-class concept**  
No major framework ships with scenario-based testing built in. LangSmith offers evaluation but it's a paid add-on. The StrongDM Dark Factory model [Source: https://www.strongdm.com/blog/the-strongdm-software-factory-building-software-with-ai] shows the market is waking up to this: "humans define intent → scenario-based validation → agents converge." This is exactly the missing layer.

**C. Quality gates between phases**  
Every framework assumes the output of phase N is acceptable input to phase N+1. Nobody builds in "grader" phases, red-team reviewers, or fact-checker phases as native pipeline constructs. These are bolted on externally if at all.

**D. Hardware-minimal deployment**  
All major frameworks assume cloud or at least a reasonably powerful dev machine. Running on a Raspberry Pi with real pipelines (2100+ tests) is genuinely unusual and positions the tool well for edge deployment and resource-constrained environments.

**E. Multi-executor model (Anthropic / OpenClaw / Gemini in one pipeline)**  
LangGraph supports multiple LLMs but they're invoked through LangChain abstractions. No one has first-class support for mixing OpenClaw (agentic platform) + Anthropic Claude + Gemini within a single pipeline definition in a way that's stable and testable.

---

## 4. Our Competitive Edge

**Genuine differentiators (honest assessment):**

1. **YAML-first pipeline definition** — The closest competitor (Promptflow) is dead. This is a real gap we fill. It makes pipelines readable, auditable, version-controllable without Python expertise.

2. **Native scenario testing** — 2100+ tests is not marketing. It means the orchestrator itself is tested. No competitor can say this about their core engine.

3. **Phase-level quality gates** — The architecture explicitly supports grader/red-team/fact-check phases as first-class pipeline stages. This maps directly to the StrongDM "validation replaces code review" insight.

4. **"Docker Compose for AI pipelines" positioning** — Immediately legible to any developer. You know what Docker Compose does. This is that, but for AI workflows. It's an analogy that sells itself.

5. **Intent preservation by design** — Prompts live in YAML, not scattered across Python files. The intent of each phase is a first-class artifact.

**Where we're weaker (honest):**
- Community size: near-zero vs LangGraph's substantial GitHub community
- LangSmith-equivalent observability: not yet built
- RAG/retrieval capabilities: not our focus; Haystack and Dify beat us here
- Enterprise integrations: Microsoft Agent Framework has deep Azure/AD/Teams integration we don't

---

## 5. Positioning Statement

The orchestration engine is the first YAML-declarative AI pipeline runner with scenario testing built in — not as a paid add-on, not as an afterthought, but as the foundational architecture. Where LangGraph requires Python expertise and LangSmith requires a credit card, the orchestration engine requires only a YAML file and a test folder. It's Docker Compose for AI pipelines: if you can read it, you can run it, version it, and trust it.

---

## 6. Additional Research Synthesis

### Jennifer Aaker Humor Framework — Application to Our Content Strategy

**Core findings:**  
[Source: https://www.gsb.stanford.edu/insights/make-em-laugh-how-humor-can-be-secret-weapon-your-communication]

Aaker (Stanford GSB) and Naomi Bagdonas ("Humor, Seriously") establish that humor:
- Builds trust and perceived competence (not just likability)
- Increases perceived confidence and elevates status attribution
- Is a **teachable skill**, not a personality trait
- Is the most underappreciated asset in professional communication

**Application to our product and content:**

1. **Technical docs can have a voice.** "Docker Compose for AI pipelines" is already a humor-adjacent analogy — it disarms by being obvious. Lean into this.

2. **The "2100+ tests" is a punchline setup.** "We have 2100 tests. Most of them pass." This is the kind of self-aware humor that signals confidence, not fragility.

3. **The YAML positioning has inherent levity.** The whole ecosystem debates whether to use LangGraph or CrewAI, and then we show up with "have you tried reading it?" That's structurally funny because it punctures a complicated argument with a simple question.

4. **For LinkedIn content:** Aaker's research suggests self-deprecating humor increases trust more than bragging. A post about "what I got wrong building an AI pipeline orchestrator" will outperform "look what we built." Pair capability with vulnerability.

5. **For article tone:** Technical articles don't have to read like RFCs. A pipeline failure story told with "Rube Goldberg machine" energy (as used in the Intent Engineering article) scores higher on trust and engagement per Aaker's research than a dry case study.

### StrongDM Dark Factory Methodology

**Core findings:**  
[Source: https://www.strongdm.com/blog/the-strongdm-software-factory-building-software-with-ai, https://law.stanford.edu/2026/02/08/built-by-agents-tested-by-agents-trusted-by-whom/]

StrongDM's "Software Factory":
- 3-person team built in 3 months
- Humans define intent → agents generate code → scenario-based validation → agents iterate to convergence
- **No human code review** — validation (against real-world scenarios, Digital Twin Universe) replaces review
- Endorsed by Ethan Mollick (Wharton), Garry Tan (YC), Simon Willison
- Represents "Level 5: The Dark Factory" in Dan Shapiro's AI-assisted programming taxonomy

**Application to our positioning:**

The Dark Factory model validates our entire architecture thesis:
1. **Humans define intent** → This is our YAML pipeline definition layer
2. **Agents execute** → This is our multi-executor (Claude/Gemini/OpenClaw) runner
3. **Scenario-based validation** → This is our scenario testing (2100+ tests, `orch scenario` command)
4. **Iterate to convergence** → This is what quality gates enable

⚠️ **Content opportunity:** A LinkedIn article positioning the orchestration engine as "the infrastructure layer that enables Dark Factory development" would be extremely timely (February 2026, peak interest in this topic). The narrative arc: "StrongDM proved the model. Here's the tooling that makes it accessible."

---

## 7. Recommended Narrative Angles

### Angle A: "Promptflow is dead. Long live YAML."
Microsoft killed the one framework that understood declarative AI pipeline definition. The gap is open. Here's why YAML won in DevOps and why it'll win in AI pipelines.
- **Supporting evidence:** Promptflow maintenance mode announcement; Docker Compose analogy; git-friendly audit trail

### Angle B: "The Dark Factory needs scaffolding"
StrongDM showed humans-define-intent → agents-execute → scenario-validates. That model requires tooling. Here's what that tooling looks like.
- **Supporting evidence:** StrongDM methodology; our scenario testing architecture; quality gates as validation layer

### Angle C: "2100 tests and zero observability debt"
The AI framework world is full of "move fast, break things." We went the other direction. Here's what it costs and what it's worth.
- **Supporting evidence:** 2100+ tests on the orchestrator itself; quality gates architecture; honest tradeoff analysis

### Angle D: "Why I stopped writing Python and started writing YAML"
Personal builder story. The moment you realize the pipeline prompt IS the product, and it shouldn't live in a .py file.
- **Supporting evidence:** Intent preservation concept; auditable YAML; version control friendliness

### Angle E: "The framework you've never heard of that runs on a Raspberry Pi"
Lightweight, runs anywhere. Most AI frameworks assume you have a server. We don't.
- **Supporting evidence:** Raspberry Pi deployment; minimal dependencies; Docker Compose analogy

## Engineering Note: Why This Phase Received an Empty Prompt

The orchestration engine spawned this subagent with `"prompt": ""`. Root cause analysis:

The `competitive_analysis` phase prompt template contains `{market_scan.output}`. The `_build_phase_input()` method wraps phase outputs in `_PhaseOutput` objects. If `market_scan` has not run yet (no entry in `self.phase_outputs`), the `_SafeDict.__missing__` method returns `<MISSING:market_scan>` — so the prompt should NOT be empty, it should contain the placeholder text.

The empty prompt suggests one of:
1. The pipeline was invoked with `--phases competitive_analysis` (skipping market_scan), and the prompt template was somehow empty
2. The OpenClaw executor is passing `task_spec.payload["prompt"]` but the OpenClaw subagent spawn mechanism is not forwarding it to the subagent context correctly
3. The `phase.prompt_template` for `competitive_analysis` was empty at time of execution (possible YAML load error)

**Recommendation:** Add a guard in `PhaseSequencer._execute_and_wait()` to raise an error if `phase_input` is empty and the prompt template is non-empty. This prevents silent data loss in the executor→subagent handoff.

The engine runs coding pipelines, security audits, UX audits, content pipelines, and research pipelines. It supports three executors: Anthropic API direct, OpenClaw sub-agents, and Gemini via local proxy. It runs on minimal hardware including a Raspberry Pi. I wrote about the Dark Factory Gap on my blog at connylazo.com. The translation incident taught me I was wrong about the edge of my competence — that's where the red team phase came from. The 24-hour cooldown exists because time reliably changes my mind about things I was certain of. I use Claude Haiku 4.5 for mechanical tasks, Sonnet 4.6 for research and writing, Opus 4.6 for editorial judgment. I built a competitive research pipeline and an editorial rewrite pipeline today — using the orchestrator to improve the orchestrator. The engine is going open source. I used Gemini to generate 100+ user stories in an hour by uploading the right context — the model wasn't smarter, the architecture was right. My content pipeline has gone through versions 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, and 2.4 — each version added because something went wrong in the previous one.
